questions.question: The First Battle of Panipat (1526) was fought between:
questions.explanation: The First Battle of Panipat (1526) was fought between Babur (founder of Mughal Empire) and Ibrahim Lodi (last ruler of Delhi Sultanate). Babur's victory established Mughal rule in India.

questions.question: Tipu Sultan was the ruler of which kingdom?
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan (1750–1799) was the Sultan of Mysore. He was called the 'Tiger of Mysore' for his fierce resistance against British expansion. He died in the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War.

questions.question: Which battle in 1565 CE decisively ended the Vijayanagara Empire?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Talikota (1565) was fought between the Vijayanagara Empire and the Deccan Sultanates (Bijapur, Bidar, Ahmadnagar, Golconda). The decisive defeat led to the sack of the Vijayanagara capital.

questions.question: The 'Third Battle of Panipat' (1761) was fought between:
questions.explanation: The Third Battle of Panipat (14 Jan 1761) between Ahmad Shah Durrani (Afghan king) and the Maratha Confederacy resulted in Maratha defeat, ending their ambition of becoming the dominant power in India.

questions.question: Bal Gangadhar Tilak gave the slogan:
questions.explanation: 'Swarajya is my birthright and I shall have it' was the famous slogan of Bal Gangadhar Tilak, the fiery nationalist who also started the Ganapati and Shivaji festivals for national awakening.

questions.question: Hampi was the capital of:
questions.explanation: Hampi (Karnataka) was the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire. It is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site with magnificent ruins including Virupaksha Temple, Vittala Temple, and palace structures.

questions.question: Lord Hastings was Governor-General during the:
questions.explanation: Lord Hastings (Warren Hastings, 1772-1785) was the first Governor-General. Lord Francis Rawdon-Hastings (1813-1823) fought the Third Anglo-Maratha War. Warren Hastings fought the First Anglo-Maratha War.

questions.question: Sher Shah Suri introduced the currency called:
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri (1540-1545) introduced the silver rupee (rupiya) weighing 178 grains. He also introduced copper paisa and gold mohur — currency reforms that continued under Mughals.

questions.question: Who was the author of 'Baburnama'?
questions.explanation: Baburnama (Tuzuk-i-Baburi) was written by Babur himself in Chagatai Turkish — it is the first autobiography in Islamic literary tradition, describing his life, campaigns, and observations of India.

questions.question: Which city was called 'City of Victory' after the Battle of Panipat 1526?
questions.explanation: After the First Battle of Panipat, Babur captured Agra and made it his capital. Delhi was also used. The battle didn't create a new city; Babur ruled from existing cities.

questions.question: The Tabaqat-i-Nasiri was written by:
questions.explanation: Tabaqat-i-Nasiri by Minhaj-i-Siraj Juzjani (1260 CE) is a valuable source of history of early Delhi Sultans, covering rulers up to Balban's period.

questions.question: The Battle of Khanwa (1527) was between:
questions.explanation: Battle of Khanwa (17 March 1527): Babur defeated Rana Sanga (Rana Sangram Singh) of Mewar, who had formed a coalition of Rajput chiefs to challenge Babur.

questions.question: Akbar defeated Hemu in:
questions.explanation: Second Battle of Panipat (5 November 1556): Akbar's general Bairam Khan defeated and killed Hemu (the last Hindu emperor to sit on Delhi's throne briefly), securing Mughal rule.

questions.question: The treaty that ended the Third Anglo-Mysore War was:
questions.explanation: Treaty of Seringapatam (1792) ended the Third Anglo-Mysore War — Tipu Sultan surrendered half his territory and paid large indemnity. The Fourth War (1799) ended with Tipu's death.

questions.question: Tipu Sultan died defending:
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan (Sultan of Mysore) died on 4 May 1799 defending his capital Srirangapatna against British forces. His death ended the kingdom of Mysore.

questions.question: The Mughal emperor who was also called 'Zinda Pir' (Living Saint):
questions.explanation: Aurangzeb was called 'Zinda Pir' (Living Saint) for his piety — he fasted regularly, never drank alcohol, lived simply, and supplemented his income by copying Qurans and making prayer caps.

questions.question: The battle that finally ended Maratha power was:
questions.explanation: Third Anglo-Maratha War (1817-18) battles of Kirkee, Korgaon, and Sitabaldi ended Maratha power. The Peshwai was abolished, Peshwa Baji Rao II exiled, making British supreme in India.

questions.question: In Indian history, 'Second Battle of Tarain (1192)' established:
options: Permanent Muslim rule in North India
questions.explanation: Second Battle of Tarain (1192) — Muhammad Ghori's defeat of Prithviraj Chauhan — established permanent Muslim rule in North India, beginning the Delhi Sultanate period.

questions.question: The Bahmani Kingdom was founded by:
questions.explanation: Alauddin Bahman Shah (Hassan Gangu, 1347-1358) founded the Bahmani Kingdom after revolting against Muhammad bin Tughluq of Delhi. It split into 5 Deccan Sultanates after 1518.

questions.question: Akbar's 'Sulh-i-Kul' policy means:
questions.explanation: Sulh-i-Kul (Peace with all/Absolute peace) was Akbar's policy of universal tolerance — treating all religions equally, respecting all faiths without discrimination.

questions.question: Iltutmish is considered the true founder of the Delhi Sultanate because:
options: He consolidated the Sultanate, got recognition from Caliph of Baghdad, completed Qutb Minar, and introduced iqta system
questions.explanation: Iltutmish (1210–1236) got recognition from the Abbasid Caliph (Islamic legitimacy), introduced the iqta system (land revenue assignments for military service), completed the Qutb Minar (started by Aibak), and introduced silver tanka and copper jital coins.

questions.question: The Battle of Talikota (1565) resulted in:
options: Vijayanagara victory over the Deccan Sultanates
Destruction of the Vijayanagara Empire — the capital Hampi was sacked and ruined
questions.explanation: The Battle of Talikota (Rakshasatangadi, 1565) was fought between Vijayanagara (Aliya Rama Raya) and the allied Deccan Sultanates (Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, Bidar, Golconda). Rama Raya was killed, Hampi was devastated and never recovered.

questions.question: Portuguese traveller Domingo Paes visited Vijayanagara during the reign of:
questions.explanation: Domingo Paes (Portuguese traveller, c. 1520–1522) visited during Krishna Deva Raya's reign and described Vijayanagara as 'the best provided city in the world' with a population of 500,000. Other visitors: Abdur Razzak (1443), Nuniz (1535–36).

questions.question: Sher Shah Suri is famous for:
options: Introducing the Grand Trunk Road, administrative reforms, land revenue system (zabt), and silver rupee
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri (1540–1545) defeated Humayun, reorganised the empire: Grand Trunk Road (Peshawar to Bengal), silver rupee (precursor to modern rupee), land revenue based on measurement (zabt), relay postal system, and built Sher Shah mosque.

questions.question: The founder of the Slave Dynasty was:
questions.explanation: Qutb-ud-din Aibak (1206-1210 CE) founded the Slave (Mamluk) Dynasty — the first Muslim dynasty in India. He was a slave of Muhammad Ghori who rose to become a sultan.

questions.question: The first woman ruler of the Delhi Sultanate was:
questions.explanation: Razia Sultana (1236-1240 CE), daughter of Iltutmish, was the first woman ruler of the Delhi Sultanate. She discarded purdah and held court openly.

questions.question: The 'iqta' system in Delhi Sultanate was similar to:
options: Feudal land grants for military service
questions.explanation: Iqta was a grant of land revenue (not land itself) to military officers (iqtadars) in lieu of salary, in return for maintaining troops and providing military service.

questions.question: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded by:
questions.explanation: Harihara I and Bukka I (brothers, disciples of Vidyaranya), formerly officers of the Kakatiyas, founded the Vijayanagara Empire in 1336 CE on the banks of Tungabhadra.

questions.question: The greatest ruler of Vijayanagara Empire was:
questions.explanation: Krishnadevaraya (1509-1529 CE) was the greatest ruler — a brilliant military commander, poet (wrote Amuktamalyada in Telugu), and generous patron of arts. Known as Kannada Rajya Rama Ramana.

questions.question: Abdur Razzaq, the Persian ambassador, visited which Vijayanagara king's court?
questions.explanation: Abdur Razzaq (Persian envoy, 1443) visited the court of Devaraya II and gave a detailed description of Vijayanagara city and the empire's grandeur.

questions.question: Battle of Talikota (1565) ended:
questions.explanation: Battle of Talikota (Jan 1565): Vijayanagara defeated by the combined forces of Bijapur, Bidar, Ahmadnagar, and Golconda. The city of Vijayanagara was sacked and destroyed.

questions.question: Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat in:
questions.explanation: First Battle of Panipat (21 April 1526): Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi using the Tulughma strategy and artillery, establishing the Mughal Empire in India.

questions.question: The Mughal emperor famous for building Taj Mahal:
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan (1628-1658) built the Taj Mahal at Agra (1632-1643) as a mausoleum for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.question: Aurangzeb reimposed the 'Jizya' tax which had been abolished by:
questions.explanation: Akbar abolished the discriminatory Jizya (poll tax on non-Muslims) in 1564 CE as part of his policy of religious tolerance. Aurangzeb reimposed it in 1679 CE.

questions.question: Todar Mal was the Finance Minister of:
questions.explanation: Raja Todar Mal was Akbar's revenue minister who introduced the Zabti (measurement) system of land revenue — measuring land and fixing revenue based on soil type and cultivation.

questions.question: The 'Mansabdari system' was a Mughal system of:
questions.explanation: Mansabdari system (introduced by Akbar) graded military and civil officials (mansabdars) by numerical ranks (mansabs) determining salary and cavalry obligation.

questions.question: Shivaji's administrative divisions were called:
questions.explanation: Shivaji's kingdom was called Swarajya (self-rule). It was divided into prants (provinces), with the capital at Raigad (Raigarh Fort).

questions.question: The Battle of Haldighati (1576) was fought between:
questions.explanation: Battle of Haldighati (18 June 1576) was fought between Maharana Pratap of Mewar and the Mughal forces led by Man Singh I on Akbar's behalf. Pratap was defeated but never surrendered.

questions.question: The Battle of Buxar (1764) was significant because:
options: British defeated combined armies of three powers
First use of firearms in India
questions.explanation: Battle of Buxar (22 October 1764): British under Hector Munro defeated the combined forces of Mir Qasim (Bengal), Shuja-ud-Daula (Awadh), and Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II — establishing British dominance.

questions.question: The 'Ain-i-Akbari' was written by:
questions.explanation: Ain-i-Akbari (Institutes of Akbar) was written by Abul Fazl, Akbar's court historian. It is the third volume of Akbarnama, providing detailed accounts of Akbar's administration, policies, and the Mughal court.

questions.question: Akbar's 'Ain-i-Akbari' and 'Akbarnama' were written by:
questions.explanation: Abul Fazl (1551–1602), a minister and historian, wrote Akbarnama (in 3 volumes, including the Ain-i-Akbari). The Ain-i-Akbari is a detailed account of Akbar's administration, court, culture, and statistics of the empire.

questions.question: The Mughal land revenue system during Akbar's reign was called:
options: Dahsala or Zabti system (introduced by Todar Mal)
questions.explanation: Raja Todar Mal introduced the Dahsala (ten-year average) system: land was measured (zabt), soil quality classified into 4 types, and revenue fixed for 10 years based on average produce. It replaced earlier arbitrary systems.
